_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 8-9 Helicopter Utilization (Revised 1/5/2023, MDHHS Reviewed 2023). Base Michigan template — MCA-specific selections not configured.
protocol_reference: Michigan System Protocol Section 8-9: Helicopter Utilization — MDHHS Approved 1/5/2023
purpose: Govern criteria for activating air medical (helicopter) transport and establish safe landing zone (LZ) procedures. Early activation is encouraged for appropriate patients — air medical does not need to be confirmed to activate; cancel if patient does not meet criteria after assessment. Scene safety and LZ management are responsibilities of the requesting ground crew.

sections.title: Clinical Criteria for Helicopter Activation
points: TRAUMA: GCS ≤ 13; penetrating injury to head/neck/torso; major burns (> 20% BSA or inhalation injury); traumatic arrest; amputation proximal to wrist/ankle; open or depressed skull fracture; suspected spinal cord injury; multiple long bone fractures; paralysis
CARDIAC: STEMI with prolonged transport to PCI-capable facility (MCA-defined threshold); refractory cardiac arrest
NEUROLOGICAL: suspected stroke with favorable LKW (Last Known Well) within thrombolytic or thrombectomy window when ground transport time would exceed benefit threshold
OB: high-risk delivery in remote location; eclampsia with hemodynamic compromise; preterm delivery < 32 weeks with prolonged ground transport
GENERAL: remote or difficult access scene; prolonged extrication; patient requiring ALS care unavailable on ground; mass casualty incident (for patient redistribution)

sections.title: Early Activation
points: Activate air medical EARLY — air medical can be cancelled if patient does not meet criteria
Do NOT wait to fully assess patient before activating — activate based on mechanism and dispatch information when appropriate

Cancel air medical if: patient condition is stable and does not meet criteria after assessment; ground transport is faster to appropriate facility; air medical is not operationally safe (weather, night)
Early activation reduces scene time and improves patient outcomes when air medical is needed

sections.title: Landing Zone (LZ) Preparation
points: MINIMUM LZ SIZE: 100 x 100 feet (day); 125 x 125 feet (night)
LZ surface: firm, level, clear of debris, wires, and poles
Mark the LZ: place markers at corners (visible to crew from above); avoid lights that blind the pilot
HAZARD IDENTIFICATION: identify and communicate to air medical crew — power lines, tall structures, towers, water towers, treetops, sloping terrain
Clear personnel and bystanders from LZ — establish a 100-foot safety perimeter
One ground crew member should face into the wind to indicate wind direction to inbound aircraft
DO NOT approach the aircraft until crew signals it is safe — NEVER approach from the tail rotor side
Extinguish all non-essential vehicle lights during approach and landing

sections.title: Patient Packaging for Air Medical
points: Ground crew packages patient before air medical arrives when possible
Secure all IV lines, tubes, and equipment before loading
Complete all critical interventions on scene — do not delay interventions anticipating air medical
Brief air medical crew on patient status, interventions, and response to treatment upon arrival

mca_selections_required.description: MCA may define specific ground transport time thresholds for air medical activation (e.g., STEMI or stroke time to appropriate facility)
options: MCA-defined transport time thresholds for air medical activation
Clinical criteria only — no time threshold specified

deterioration_flags: Weather or visibility deteriorating — air medical may decline or abort; continue ground transport to appropriate facility
LZ unsafe or unsuitable — identify alternate LZ; do NOT proceed with unsafe LZ
scope_notes: Activate EARLY — cancel if patient does not meet criteria
Criteria: GCS ≤13 trauma, penetrating torso, burns >20%, traumatic arrest, proximal amputation, STEMI with prolonged transport, stroke in window, OB emergency
LZ: 100x100 ft day, 125x125 ft night; level, clear, firm
DO NOT approach from tail rotor
Ground crew responsible for LZ safety
